label: ai
topic: A man cloned from 10000-year-old DNA on a space station discovers his origins
text:
A crevasse is a crack in a glacier. A glacier is a river of ice that moves too slowly for the human eye to perceive. A man who falls into a crevasse and is preserved by the cold may, under the right conditions, remain biologically intact for longer than the glacier itself.

This is not a metaphor. This is what happened.

Ten thousand years before the Accord Ring was built, before the elevators were anchored and the habitation modules were pressurised, a man walked across a frozen plain and did not come back. His name is lost. Everything about him is lost except his cells, which were recovered from a core sample in the twenty-third century and catalogued in the Svalbard Biological Archive under reference number SVB-0091-H.

The archive was not supposed to be used for reproduction. The archive was supposed to be a record.

Del found out on a Wednesday.

The medical technician who told him was named Priya. She was twenty-six years old, she had been in the job for four months, and she was clearly drawing on reserves of professional composure that were not yet fully stocked.

"So I'm a clone," Del said.

Priya winced. "That's not exactly the terminology we use."

"What terminology do you use?"

"You're a genetically derived individual from a preserved biological sample." She paused. "But colloquially, yes. Essentially."

Del was sitting in a plastic chair in a corridor outside the haematology suite on Level Fourteen of Wheel West. The corridor smelled of antiseptic and recycled air, and through the window he could see the curve of the ring extending away in both directions, habitation modules strung along it like beads on a wire. Eight million people lived on the Accord Ring. Nineteen of them shared his genetic origin.

"Nineteen others," he said. "Can I meet them?"

"Privacy regulations," Priya said. "But if they consent to contact, we can facilitate introductions."

"Were any of us supposed to exist?"

Priya looked at her tablet. "The unauthorised insemination event occurred twenty-nine years ago. A researcher named Hakim Osei accessed the Svalbard archive, extracted twenty samples, and introduced them into the standard incubation queue at the Wheel West Obstetrics Facility. He was apprehended, tried, and sentenced to community service."

"Community service."

"It was a different policy environment."

A story, according to the classical definition, is a sequence of events connected by causality. A man falls into a crevasse. Ten thousand years pass. A researcher opens a freezer. A boy grows up on a space station, not knowing that the cells in his body were assembled from instructions written before the invention of agriculture.

But a story also requires a reason for telling. What makes Del's situation a story rather than a medical file is the question it forces him to confront: whether identity is carried in the body or constructed by experience, and whether it matters that the original owner of his genome died alone in ice that has since melted.

Del thought about this for several days. He went to work at the water reclamation plant on Level Seven. He ate meals in the communal kitchen. He watched ships dock at the elevator terminus and undock again, and he ran his thumb across the inside of his wrist where Priya had drawn blood, feeling the small bruise that was the only physical evidence of what he had learned.

On the fifth day, he submitted a request to the Svalbard Archive for information about SVB-0091-H.

The file was brief. Male. Estimated age at death: thirty to thirty-five. Cause of death: exposure. Location of recovery: sixty-seven degrees north latitude, fourteen degrees east longitude, depth of nine metres within glacial ice.

No name. No possessions. No indication of why he had been walking across a glacier alone or where he had been going.

Del read the file twice. Then he closed it and went to the observation deck on Level Twenty, where the ring's rotation created a simulated horizon, and he looked out at the star field that had existed before the man who made him and would exist long after the ring that held him, and he thought: I am here because someone fell.
